coredata.dc:title: Solvent-dependent red emissive carbon dots and their applications in sensing and solid-state luminescence
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wenjing
Wu, Jing
Xing, Yuxin
Wang, Zonghua
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a kind of red emissive carbon dots (BNCDs) based on 1,1′-binaphthyl-2,2′-diamine (BNDM) and citric acid as carbon source were prepared through one-pot solvothermal method. The synthesized BNCDs have uniform size distribution, photobleaching resistance, and excitation wavelength-independent emission performance with the optimal fluorescence at λex/λem = 540/603 nm. The composite films of BNCDs in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix exhibited bright red emission under ultraviolet irradiation, indicating their potential in solid-state luminescence. Also, BNCDs shows unique solvent-dependent phenomenon, the optical behavior can be tuned by the change of solvent polarity. Another striking observation was that BNCDs can be applied as a fluorescence sensor for highly sensitive quantitative detection of trace water in organic solvents because it’s polarity-sensitive property. Through fluorescence “on–off–on” mechanism, BNCDs could be used as a fluorescence sensor for detection of Fe3+ and F- with LOD of 0.219 and 0.0104 μmol L−1, respectively. This strategy offers an efficient approach for preparing red emissive carbon dots and lays a foundation for its multi-functional application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red emission
Solvent-dependent
Solid-state luminescence
Sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast doctopic grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of binaphthyl based carbon dots (BNCDs)
Detection of water in organic solvents
Fluorescence detection of Fe3 + and F−
Effect of solvent polarity on the optical spectra of BNCDs
Detection of water content in organic solvents
Fluorescence response of BNCDs toward pH
Fe3+ and F- detection of BNCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM images and (b) histograms of particles size distribution of BNCDs; (c) AFM images and (d) height analysis of BNCDs.
FT-IR spectra of BNDM and BNCDs.
(a) XPS spectra and (b) C1s, (c) O1s, (d) N1s peak of the as-prepared BNCDs.
Photographs of BNCDs/PVA composite films under the irradiation of daylight (a) and UV light (b).

(a) The normalized fluorescence spectra of BNCDs (insert: the images of BNCDs in different solvents under 540 nm excitation (from left to right: toluene, EA, THF, acetone, DCM, DMF, ACN, DMSO, EtOH, MeOH)) and (b) the normalized absorption spectra in different solvents at room temperature; (c) The linear relationship between the optimal fluorescence emission wavelength and the optimal UV–vis absorption wavelength and solvent polarity.
Fluorescence spectra of BNCDs with different water contents in (a) EtOH, (b) ACN, (c) THF and (d) MeOH and the linear correlations between the fluorescence intensity ratios and water content.

(a-b) The ﬂuorescence spectra of the BNCDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ and the linear relationship between ∆F/F0 and Fe3+; (c-d) The ﬂuorescence spectra of the BNCDs–Fe3+ system in the presence of different concentrations of F- and the linear relationship between ∆F/F0 and F-.
The polarity parameters of various solvents and the fluorescence emission wavelength and the UV–visible absorption wavelength of BNCDs.
Comparison of BNCDs with other reported CDs in water detection.

head.ce:title: Solvent-dependent red emissive carbon dots and their applications in sensing and solid-state luminescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong Sino-Japanese Center for Collaborative Research of Carbon Nanomaterials, Instrumental Analysis Center of Qingdao University, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong Sino-Japanese Center for Collaborative Research of Carbon Nanomaterials, Instrumental Analysis Center of Qingdao University, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong Sino-Japanese Center for Collaborative Research of Carbon Nanomaterials, Instrumental Analysis Center of Qingdao University, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a kind of red emissive carbon dots (BNCDs) based on 1,1′-binaphthyl-2,2′-diamine (BNDM) and citric acid as carbon source were prepared through one-pot solvothermal method. The synthesized BNCDs have uniform size distribution, photobleaching resistance, and excitation wavelength-independent emission performance with the optimal fluorescence at λex/λem = 540/603 nm. The composite films of BNCDs in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix exhibited bright red emission under ultraviolet irradiation, indicating their potential in solid-state luminescence. Also, BNCDs shows unique solvent-dependent phenomenon, the optical behavior can be tuned by the change of solvent polarity. Another striking observation was that BNCDs can be applied as a fluorescence sensor for highly sensitive quantitative detection of trace water in organic solvents because it’s polarity-sensitive property. Through fluorescence “on–off–on” mechanism, BNCDs could be used as a fluorescence sensor for detection of Fe3+ and F- with LOD of 0.219 and 0.0104 μmol L−1, respectively. This strategy offers an efficient approach for preparing red emissive carbon dots and lays a foundation for its multi-functional application.
Solvent-dependent carbon dots with red emissive and their applications in multi-sensing and solid-state luminescence.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a quasi-0D carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials with a size below 10 nm, have attracted tremendous attention due to their excellent photoluminescent property, low toxicity, biocompatibility, high resistance to photobleaching, and easy to functionalization [1–3]. These excellent properties make them play a great role in the fields of sensing [4–6], bioimaging [7–9], photocatalysis [10] and optoelectronic devices [11–13]. In early studies, the maximum emission of the prepared CDs was limited to the blue or green region. CDs with various emission colors (white [11,12], yellow [4,5], yellow-green [14], orange [15,16], red [6,17,18] or infra-red (NIR) [13]) have been successfully developed in recent decades.

The optical property of CDs can be tuned via adjusting the synthesis conditions and reagents during the process of synthesis, e.g. by choosing proper carbon precursor [6,7], heteroatom (nitrogen, sulfur, or fluoride) doping [10,13], or using different solvents as reaction medium [19]. In addition, assorted post-treatment strategies are introduced to tune the emission wavelength by regulating excitation wavelength [20], pH, solvent [8], concentration [21,22], pressure [23] or separation by silica column chromatography [24] of the prepared CDs. Among these strategies, excitation-dependent phenomenon has been commonly observed in many CDs, but the shift of emission wavelength is often accompanied by decline in fluorescence intensity [20]. Significant progress in the development of CDs exhibiting solvent-dependent property behavior [8,25–28]. For example, Shi et al. reported that by using p-phenylenediamine as precursor, red color-emitting CDs were prepared. The photoluminescence color of the CDs is varied in different solvent with the increasing of polarity [8]. Stepanidenko et al. investigated the influence of the surrounding solvent media on the optical properties of CDs synthesized from citric acid and ethylenediamine, found that the CDs emission originates from several luminescent centers of different types [29]. o-CDs were synthesized by simple one-step hydrothermal treatment of o-phenylenediamine. o-CDs showed different emission colors in different solvents at one single excitation wavelength. The emission peak of the as-prepared o-CDs was sensitive to the water in organic solvents [25]. Nitrogen-doped CDs were synthesized by using ninhydrin and o-phenylenediamine as precursor, the CDs exhibit green, yellow, orange and red luminescence in DMF, ethyl alcohol, water and acetic acid, respectively [30].

However, as reported that most red emissive solvent-dependent CDs were prepared from phenylenediamine isomer. The red emission came from the nitrogen-containing fluorophores and the aromatic ring structure of phenylenediamine isomer. The solvent-dependent fluorescence property was treated as dipole induced surface electronic state change [27]. Based on these facts, 1,1′-binaphthyl-2,2′-diamine (BNDM), which has π–π conjugation aromatic ring structure and amino groups, could reduce the bandgap and achieve long-wavelength emission. This motivated us to undertake BNDM as a novel carbon precursor to prepare new kinds of red emissive solvent-dependent CDs.

In this work, we report the synthesis of multi-functional, red emissive CDs by a simple solvothermal method using BNDM as carbon source. Based on the fluorescence “on–off–on” mechanism, the method of detection Fe3+ and F- was established. We explored the interaction of BNCDs with the solvents, toluene, ethyl acetate, tetrahydrofuran, acetone, dichloromethane, N, N-dimethylformamide, acetonitrile, dimethyl sulfoxide, ethanol, and methanol to clarify their tunable fluorescence properties. Additionally, the excellent solvation effects of BNCDs have prospects in the detection of water content in organic solvents. We further fabricated the BNCDs/PVA composite films, which can be potential used in solid-state luminescence.

Citric acid and toluene were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). 1,1′-Binaphthyl-2,2′-diamine (BNDM) and tris(hydroxymethyl)aminomethane were from Aladdin. Various ions (Na3PO4, NaBr, NaF, Na2S, HClO, HCl, NaNO3, NaI, NaAC, CaCl2, KCl, NaCl, Na2SO4, Na2SO3, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, CuCl2, Cd(NO3)2, BaCl2, Zn(NO3)2, MgCl2, FeCl3, HCl) were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). Toluene (Toluene), ethyl acetate (EA), dichloromethane (DCM), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone (Acetone), acetonitrile (ACN), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol (EtOH) and methanol (MeOH) are of HPLC level. Other reagents were at least analytical grade and used as received without further purification. The experimental water was secondary deionized water (18 MΩ cm).

The size and morphology of BNCDs were characterized in JEM-1200EX transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The height of BNCDs were characterized by using CSPM4000 atomic force microscope (AFM) (Benyuan Co. Ltd., Guangzhou, China). The structure of BNCDs were performed on Nicolet iS50 instrument (Thermo Scientific, Madison, WI, USA) in the range of 500–4000 cm−1 wavenumbers and Kratos XSAM 800 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (Thermo Scientific, Madison, WI, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was detected by D/MAX 2500/PC X-ray powder diffractometer (Rigaku Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation. Raman spectra were obtained with a laser excitation of 532 nm on DXR2 Raman Spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Madison, WI, USA). UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were performed on UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Tokyo, Japan) and F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Tokyo, Japan), respectively. FLS 1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., England) was applied to measure fluorescence decay curves.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of binaphthyl based carbon dots (BNCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Binaphthyl based red emission carbon dots were synthesized by a facile solvothermal method ( Scheme 1). In brief, 1,1′-binaphthyl-2,2′-diamine (0.05 g) and citric acid (0.02 g) were dissolved in 10 mL toluene solvent, then the solution was transferred into a poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 12 h in an electric oven. After the solvothermal treatment, the mixture was filtered through a filter (0.22 µm) to remove large particles and precipitation. Afterward, the prepared carbon dots were dialyzed with regenerated cellulose dialysis membranes (Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China, MWCO: 500–1000) in ethanol solution for 3 days. The purified brown carbon dots powder was finally obtained through rotary steaming and freeze-drying.

1 mL of BNCDs solution (2.0 mg mL−1, dissolved in DMSO) was mixed with 5 mL of 20% polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) solution in DMSO. After slowly stirring, a homogeneous BNCDs/PVA gel solution was obtained. Then, the homogeneous gel was coated on a glass slide substrate and dried for 72 h to obtain a BNCDs/PVA composite membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: EtOH, MeOH, ACN, and THF were selected as model organic solvents for water content detection. Different water containing solutions (0–70%) of EtOH, MeOH, ACN, and THF were dispersed in 0.20 mg mL−1 BNCDs solution. Then, the fluorescence spectra of these samples were measured at λex/λem= 540/603 nm. The excitation and emission slit widths were set at 10 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3 + and F−
ce:section.ce:para: At first, 1.0 mL BNCDs solution (0.20 mg mL−1, dissolved in 50 mL ethanol solution) was transferred into a 2.0 mL centrifuge tube and diﬀerent concentrations of Fe3+ solution was added, mixed thoroughly and equilibrated for 10 min at room temperature. Then, different concentrations of fluoride ions were added into the mixture, shake them well and equilibrate for 5 min. The fluorescence of this system was recorded at λex/λem= 540/603 nm. The excitation and emission slit widths were set at 10 nm.

The morphology of prepared carbon dots was observed by HRTEM and AFM. As displayed in Fig. 1, the BNCDs are quasi-spherical and well dispersed with relatively uniform size in the range of 1.0–3.5 nm, and the average particle size is to be ca. 2.6 nm. The AFM image shows that the topographic height of BNCDs is mainly distributed in 1.0–3.0 nm, which is consistent with the HRTEM results. From the X-ray diffraction pattern (Figure S1), the BNCDs have a broad apparent diffraction peak around 20.06°, indicating the structure of the carbon dots is amorphous. To further investigate the structure of BNCDs, Raman spectra are carried out. Two significant peaks appeared at 1374 cm−1 and 1595 cm−1, which are attributed to the disordered D-band peak (sp3 hybrid carbon) and the crystalline G-band peak (sp2 hybrid carbon). The calculated ID/IG= 0.64, indicating that BNCDs have a locally irregular graphene-like structure.

The surface functional groups and chemical bonds of BNCDs were characterized by FT-IR and XPS analysis. The FT-IR spectrum of the original material BNDM and the product BNCDs were presented in Fig. 2. For BNDM, the bands at 3033 cm–1, 2909 cm–1, and 2846 cm–1 were assigned to C–H stretching vibration of the naphthyl units, 1592, 1531, and 1500 cm−1 were ascribed to C–H bending vibration of the naphthyl units. 1452 cm−1 was CC. While the N–H vibrational peak at approximately 3291 cm−1 confirms the existence of a secondary amine. The peak at 1376 cm−1 was assigned to C–N stretching vibrations.

For BNCDs, the band at 3315 cm−1 was assigned to N–H vibrational peak, peaks at 2976 cm−1, 2928 cm−1, and 2880 cm−1 relate to methylene stretching vibrations, 877 cm−1 and 1323 cm−1 indicate C−H bending vibrations. The presence of the amide bond located at 1663 cm−1, indicating that the amine groups in BNDMs and carbonyl groups in citric acid had conjugated via amidation reaction. Peaks at 1084 cm−1 and 1046 cm−1 originate from C−O bonds, while the band at 1452 cm−1 is assigned to CC, 1271 cm−1 was aromatic C–N bond. Through amidation and the subsequent carbonization reaction of BNDM and citric acid, BNCDs is formed.

The full XPS spectrum ( Fig. 3) shows three typical peaks at 285 eV, 399 eV, 530 eV, indicating that the prepared BNCDs have C, N, and O elements. Meanwhile, the ratio of C, N, and O is 45.82%, 26.19%, and 27.99%, respectively. For the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1 s, four pronounced peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.3 eV, 287.1 eV, and 288.5 eV are considered to be C-C/CC, C-N/C-O, CN, and CO groups. The O1 s spectrum is fitted into two peaks at 531.6 eV and 533.0 eV, contributing to CO and C-O. The spectrum of N1 s in BNCDs are split into three peaks at 398.0 eV, 400.0 eV and 401.5 eV, which were ascribed to the CN-C (pyridinic N), C-N-C (pyrrolic N), and C-NH2 (amino N) groups, indicating that part of N atoms have doped into the carbon framework and the rest exist in the form of amino groups surrounding the BNCDs surface. The XPS analyses are in accordance with the FT-IR results. The presence of polyaromatic structures and amide linkage groups endow BNCDs with hydrophobicity.

Under the continuous illumination (λex/λem=540/603 nm) for 7200 s, no obvious fluorescence changes of BNCDs were observed as shown in Figure S2, indicating that the strong anti-photo bleaching and high photostability of the prepared BNCDs. When the excitation wavelength shifts from 480 nm to 560 nm, the emission wavelength was gradually red shifted (Figure S3). At 540 nm excitation, the optimal emission at 603 nm was obtained. Generally, the excitation-independent fluorescence emission phenomenon was ascribed to the particle size and the surface defect sites of BNCDs. The quantum yield of BNCDs was calculated to be 13.3% (Rhodamine B in ethanol (QY = 0.56) was chosen as a standard). The fluorescence lifetime of the BNCDs was 7.81 ns.

A possible mechanism for the luminescence of BNCDs is that the π–π conjugation aromatic structure would consequently lower the energy gap by increasing sp2 domains size, thus resulting in the emission of red luminescence. On the other hand, the N-containing groups (pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and amino N) brought surface molecular state related fluorescence.

Besides the above basic characterization and usual features, we found that when PVA was chosen as the matrix for preparing BNCDs/PVA composite films, the films have good film-forming properties and appear as a homogeneous light brown transparent film in natural light ( Fig. 4(a)) and show bright red fluorescence under UV light irradiation. This is because the large number of PVA chains covered around the BNCDs as a hindrance in the aggregation state inhibit the occurrence of π-π stacking and avoid the fluorescence quenching [31], indicating that self-quenching-resistant BNCDs have the potential in solid-state luminescence fields or applied as an optical material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solvent polarity on the optical spectra of BNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, we found that BNCDs shows different spectral behavior in different solvents. The fluorescence emission spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of the following 10 different polar solvents, such as toluene (Toluene), ethyl acetate (EA), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone (Acetone), dichloromethane (DCM), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetonitrile (ACN), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethanol (EtOH) and methanol (MeOH) were implemented. These dispersions exhibit homogeneous phases without any obvious precipitation at room temperature.

Fig. 5(a) shows the normalized fluorescence emission spectrum of BNCDs individually dispersed in 10 solvents, the optimum fluorescence emission wavelength gradually red shifted from 583 nm to 608 nm, the corresponding UV–vis absorption peak also gradually shift from 344 nm to 355 nm. Meanwhile, from the photos of BNCDs in different solvents under 540 nm excitation (Fig. 5(a) insert), the color changes gradually from orange to red as the increasing of polarity. Solvent polarity highly affects the PL emission wavelength of CDs. BNCDs shows strict solvent-dependent behavior.

To explain this solvent-dependent behavior, polarity parameters (ET N) [32] was introduced to measure the solvation capability of solvents, which was based on the specific interactions (such as hydrogen bonding, solvation, charge transfer, etc.) and non-specific interactions (e.g., dipole-dipole between molecules, dielectric constant, etc.) between the solvent and the target. To measure the normalized polarity parameter of the solvent. As shown in Table 1, the polarity parameters of various solvents (from toluene (lowest polarity) to methanol (highest polarity)) and the optimum fluorescence maximum emission peak positions and the UV–vis absorption peak position of BNCDs under the corresponding solvents are listed. The fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorption peak position respectively red-shifted by 25.2 nm (583.2 nm in toluene, 608.4 nm in MeOH) and 11 nm (344 nm in toluene, 355 nm in MeOH) without a change in shape.

As observed in Fig. 5(c), there is a good linear relationship between the emission/absorption peak position and the solvent polarity parameter of the solvent. Therefore, BNCDs can be used as an effective fluorescent probe for measuring the polarity of solvents.

The strong solvation effect can be clarified as follows. In this system, when BNCDs are dissolved in a polar solvent, the emission is affected by the hydrogen bond between the nitrogen atom on the surface of BNCDs and the solvent. The edge N structures, such as pyrrolic and pyridinic forms, can increase the charge-carrier density in a high polar solvent by attracting more charges by their easy interaction with the surrounding solvents [27]. Moreover, when the electron is excited from the ground state, it can be stabilized by changing the dipole direction of the surrounding solvent molecules. As solvent polarity increases, the energy required to change the dipole direction of the solvent molecules also increases because of the strong solvent dipole moment. While in a non-polar solvent, the dipole-dipole interaction between the molecules would affect the surface electronic state of BNCDs. The surface has different energy level structures. As a result, excited electrons in a high polar solvent lose more energy than those in the low polar solvent, which results in a red shift of the emission. In addition, internal charge-transfer and conformational changes can be caused by the hydrogen bonds between BNCDs and polar protic solvents [28,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: Water, as a common impurity in organic solvents, is important to detect its presence quantitatively and qualitatively in the chemical industries, especially in multiple-step organic synthesis. In the past few decades, Karl Fischer titration [34] (including volumetric and colorimetric Karl Fischer titration) has been widely used as a traditional method for water detection. However, the requirement of specific instruments and considerable expertise seriously limits its further application.

Based on this unique solvation effect of BNCDs, we further studied their application in the detection of water in organic solvents, wherein a sensor can be established to detect water content in organic solvents. Typically, the water response performance of BNCDs in EtOH, MeOH, ACN, and THF were explored. To prevent the sample from absorbing moisture from the air, the newly opened solvent is homogenized and analyzed immediately. The results show that BNCDs can be used as a water-sensitive fluorescent probe to detect the moisture content in organic solvents. The fluorescence intensity of BNCDs in organic solvents under different water content (0–70%) was shown in Fig. 6.

Here, taking water content (0–70%) in EtOH as an instance (Fig. 6(a)), as the water content increases, the optimum fluorescence emission wavelength gradually redshifts and its fluorescence intensity decreases. In the two linear regions with the water content of 0–10% and 10–70%, good linear relationships between the water content in ethanol and the fluorescence intensity of BNCDs were obtained. The characteristic peak change is exceedingly sensitive when the water content is less than 10%, the linear equation in the range of 1–10% is as follows: F/F0 = −0.012 × [C]+ 1.015, R2 = 0.990. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of BNCDs in the absence and presence of water, respectively. When the signal-to-noise ratio is 3, the detection limit is calculated to be 0.38%. The relative standard deviation is 1.51% (water content: 1%, n = 11). Besides EtOH, the water content can also be detected in other organic solvents (ACN (Fig. 6(b)), THF (Fig. 6(c)), and MeOH (Fig. 6(d))). The detection limits of water in ACN, THF and MeOH are 1.05%, 2.97%, 0.41%, respectively. BNCDs exhibited polarity-sensitive property, could be applied as a sensor to detect the water content in organic solvents, which provides a potential platform to determine the purity of the organic solvents. The previous literatures on CDs-based solvent sensors [25,28,35–39] are listed in Table 2 for comparison. The results show that this work was comparable to those reported work.

When trace amount of water is added to different organic solvents, on one hand, due to the low dispersion of BNCDs in water, BNCDs tend to aggregate gradually, and induce the red shift of the emission wavelength with fluorescence quenching. On the other hand, according to the XPS characterization results, there is a certain amount of pyridine nitrogen in the BNCDs structure. Because the high electron density around the pyridine nitrogen, it can be used as a hydrogen bond acceptor, thereby increasing the possibility of non-radiative relaxation of the excited carbon dots, which causes the fluorescence quenching [28]. In addition, the π-π accumulation on the surface of the carbon dots can also be used as a receptor for hydrogen bonding, which further cause the decrease of the fluorescence intensity [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of BNCDs toward pH
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of pH on the fluorescence property of BNCDs was also explored. As shown in Figure S4, as the pH value increases from 4 to 13, the emission peak positions of BNCDs are respectively 602.0 nm, 605.6 nm, 606.0 nm, 606.6 nm, 609.8 nm, 609.2 nm, 604.0 nm, 601.0 nm, 597.6 nm, 584.0 nm. At pH 4–8, the position of the fluorescence emission peak gradually redshift as the pH increasing, however, when the pH is further increased, the position shifts to blue. The reason for this phenomenon can be explained as follows. At pH 4–8, the increase in pH greatly enhances the intramolecular charge transfer characteristics of BNCDs. Under weak alkaline conditions, BNCDs tend to form enhanced intramolecular charge transfer structures (such as amide groups). As the alkalinity increases, the passivation environment on the surface of BNCDs is changed, and the emission wavelength tend to blue shift. It can be speculated that the emission wavelength of BNCDs toward pH is affected by the surface groups.

Besides, the change in pH caused the protonation and deprotonation behavior of nitrogen on the surface of BNCDs, which changed the surface electronic state, caused a decrease in fluorescence intensity. Under acidic conditions, the lone pair of nitrogen atoms form a coordination structure with H+, protonation occurs, and the electrons are captured by the charge centers on the surface of the BNCDs, electron-hole pairs forming, induce the quenching of fluorescence. However, under alkaline conditions, the nitrogen atoms on the surface of the carbon dots are deprotonated, and an electronegative "protective layer" is formed on the surface of the BNCDs to reduce the radiation recombination of the electron-hole pair and the fluorescence intensity. However, within the pH range of 7–9, the protonation/deprotonation behavior of BNCDs does not occur, and the fluorescence intensity remains at a relatively high level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ and F- detection of BNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Excess F– intake can cause dental and skeletal fluorosis on human beings’ health; it’s urgently required to develop a facile and sensitive method to F– detection. With the addition of Fe3+ in an aqueous solution, the fluorescence of BNCDs at 603 nm was significantly quenched. This phenomenon was mostly attributed to the binding affinity of the hydroxyl group on BNCDs surface to Fe3+. The formed BNCDs/Fe3+ complexes promoted electron transfer and restrained exciton recombination, which caused significant fluorescence quenching [40,41]. A nice linear relationship (R2 = 0.990) is observed between the intensity recovery ratio ∆F/F0 and Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0–10 µmol L−1 and 10–80 µmol L−1. The linear equation was fitted as ∆F/F0 = −0.0143 × [C]+ 0.0162, the detection limit was calculated to be 0.219 μmol L−1 based on three times the standard deviation rule.

The selectivity of the prepared CDs for the Fe3+ can be attributed to the faster chelating process between BNCDs and Fe3+ through N/O-containing groups [42,43]. In general, the fluorescence quenching by metal ion binding can be reversibly restored by competitive reaction [44]. In this work, the affinity between F– and Fe3+ is greater than that between Fe3+ and BNCDs, suggesting that F– could competitively capture Fe3+ from the BNCDs/Fe3+ complex and form FeF3, thus the BNCDs get freedom with fluorescence recovery. To evaluate the selectivity of BNCDs/Fe3+ towards F−, various kinds of metal ions and anions were added into the system. However, none of these control substances had obvious influences on the fluorescence response, only F− can make the fluorescence intensity regain (Figure S5). These results demonstrate the selectivity of the BNCDs towards F− over the tolerance ions. As a result, the excellent selectivity of BNCDs/Fe3+ for F− renders it the potential for the detection of F−. Furthermore, a good linearity is observed between ∆F/F0 and the concentration of F– in the range of 0.4–1.0 μmol L−1 with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.982 (insert in Fig. 7c). The detection limit for F− was calculated to be 0.0104 μmol L−1 (3σ/k，n = 11), RSD was 1.24%. NBCDs/Fe3+/F– could be developed as an effective fluorescence “on–off–on” sensor for F- detection.

A kind of solvent-dependent CDs with red fluorescence were developed from BNDM as a novel precursor via solvothermal method. BNCDs exhibited the diverse and potential applications in solid-state luminescence and multi-sensing. In 10 different solvents, BNCDs shows different optical behavior. As the solvent polarity increases, the PL emission wavelength and UV absorption wavelength shifts to longer wavelength. Based on this polarity-sensitive/solvent-dependent fluorescence effect, BNCDs can be applied as an efficient sensor to detect water content in organic solvents with low detection limit. Interestingly, the fluorescence of BNCDs could be effectively quenched by Fe3+. Subsequently, the addition of F– results in fluorescence recovery of BNCDs because the formation of FeF3 compound. Based on this phenomenon, potential applications of BNCDs for Fe3+ and F– detection can be realized. In addition, solid-state materials with bright red emission could be easily acquired via mixing PVB and BNCDs. These above characteristics indicating that BNCDs can be processed in various forms to meet the requirements of different applications. Scheme 1.

Wenjing Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Software, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Jing Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yuxin Xing: Validation, Investigation. Zonghua Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21804075), Qingdao Postdoctoral Applied Research Project, Taishan Scholar Program of Shandong Province (ts201511027) and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong (ZR2020BM063).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wenjing Wang, a lecturer of Qingdao University. Her research focuses on fluorescent nano-materials.
Jing Wu, a graduate student in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University. Her research focuses on biosensors and fluorescent nano-materials.
Yuxin Xing, a graduate student in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University. Her research focuses on biosensors and nano-materials.
Zonghua Wang, holds a position as a professor at Qingdao University. Her research focuses on biosensors and nano-materials.
